creator: jon@xyinn.org
text:
FYI, I spent a few more hours trying to debug what's going on with ntpd, and wasn't able to get it work even with the simplest configurations I mentioned earlier.

An example:

root@leslie:~ # cat /etc/ntp.conf
driftfile /var/db/ntp.drift
pool 0.freebsd.pool.ntp.org iburst

and launch the application with the -g flag.

For cross verification, I downloaded chrony and used the following config:

jon@leslie:~ $ cat /usr/local/etc/chrony.conf
pool 0.freebsd.pool.ntp.org iburst
driftfile /var/db/chrony/drift
makestep 1 3

I then went ahead and changed the click as you mentioned (date 0000), and I started chrony via 'service chronyd start'. It connected immediately and set the time.

root@leslie:~ # date 0000
Thu May 26 00:00:00 EDT 2022
root@leslie:~ # date
Thu May 26 00:00:00 EDT 2022
root@leslie:~ # service chronyd start
Starting chronyd.
root@leslie:~ # date
Thu May 26 23:53:05 EDT 2022

root@leslie:~ # chronyc sources                                                                

MS Name/IP address         Stratum Poll Reach LastRx Last sample
===============================================================================
^+ 38.100.216.142                2   6    73    30  -4162us[-4162us] +/-   92ms
^* time.richiemcintosh.com       2   6   177    37   +595us[+1110us] +/-   46ms
^+ jane.qotw.net                 2   6   177    35   -339us[ -339us] +/-   83ms
^+ ntp.speculation.org           2   6    76   164  +3457us[+2956us] +/-   92ms

I'll go ahead and switch to chrony. I believe OpenNTPD would also be another alternative from my earlier tests.
